pattern: Matching characters from both ends across the two strings peels away a guaranteed-palindromic shell, leaving only a small middle segment of either string that needs to be checked on its own.
hints: Walk inward from both ends comparing a's front character against b's back character; every match can be part of the palindrome shell regardless of which string it ultimately lands in.
Once the matching stops, the only way to finish is if the leftover middle segment of a is itself a palindrome, or the leftover middle segment of b is.
The split point isn't fixed to matching a's front against b's back -- also try matching b's front against a's back, since the palindrome could favor either string first.

edge_inputs.description: no valid split exists, statement example
valid split found only when checking b's prefix with a's suffix
a itself is already a palindrome, so any split works trivially
outer characters match all the way through with nothing left over
outer characters match partway, and only the b-side leftover happens to be a palindrome
outer characters mismatch immediately, forcing a check of the whole strings as-is

stress.description: random lowercase strings, sometimes mirrored to create matches, sizes kept moderate for the quadratic brute-force oracle
